answer:
**The Falsafa corpus contains several Dharmaśāstra texts (Smṛtis and related works) that systematically engage with rules and claims about food and dietary practices.** These include prescriptions for ritual offerings (*śrāddha*, *homa*), purity rules during impurity (*āśauca*), penances (*prāyaścitta*) involving restricted diets or fasting (*upavāsa*, *kṛcchra*), prohibitions on "forbidden foods" (e.g., from certain castes or untouchables), rules on mixing offered/unoffered food, timing of eating during eclipses or vows (*vrata*), lightness of diet (*laghu-āhāra*) as a vow (*niyama*), and detailed claims on meat consumption (permitted only in sacrifices, with lists of animals satisfying ancestors for specific durations).[^1][^2][^3][^4]

No other works in the corpus prescribe or claim rules about food/diet in this structured, normative way. Narrative or incidental mentions (e.g., cannibalistic "law" in poetry,[^5] physiological diet in economics[^6]) do not qualify as engaging with *rules or claims* in the prescriptive sense of the query.

### Works Engaging with Food/Dietary Rules
The following 10 works contain such material (all Indic, mostly Ancient/Medieval Smṛtis):

1. **Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (Ch. 1): Extensive rules on food in rituals/penances (e.g., no solid food in impurity, forbidden foods require bathing; cooked vs. uncooked in *śrāddha*; eclipse fasting; hot food offerings).[^7]
2. **Manusmṛti** (Ch. 2–6): Rules on avoiding honey/meat/perfumes; permitted meats in sacrifice (e.g., fish for 2 months' ancestral satisfaction); "eater of fish eats all meat"; abstain from meat for heaven.[^8]
3. **Vratisaṃśana** (Ch. 1): *Niyama* vows include "lightness of diet" (contentment, avoid improper foods).[^9]
4. **Yama Smṛti** (Ch. 1): Penances with barley gruel/cow urine diet for forbidden food.[^10]
5. **Bṛhaspati Smṛti** (Ch. 1): Forbidden food as legal offense (with homicide); wife avoids meat/liquor.[^11]
6. **Kātyāyana Smṛti** (Ch. 11, 68): Forbidden food offenses; meager diet in punishment.[^12]
7. **Viṣṇu Smṛti** (Ch. 22, 46, 54–55): *Āśauca* eating rules by caste; fasting penances (*parāka*: 12 days).[^13]
8. **Parāśara Smṛti** (Ch. 6–7): Fasting for killing birds/animals; *pañcagavya* after impure eating.[^14]
9. **Vīramitrodaya** (Ch. 1): Tithi rules for fasting/rites (e.g., *pūrṇaviddhā* prohibited).[^15]
10. **Cynewulf - Andreas** (Ch. 1): Claims a tribal "law" of feeding guests human flesh.[^16]

**No coverage in the other 27 works** (e.g., no rules in poetry like Ghalib/Iqbal or economics like Dunoyer/Comte beyond physiology).

[^1]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 5 (meat rules summary). [Chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/).
[^2]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1 (food in *śrāddha*). [Chapter](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/).
[^3]: Vratisaṃśana, Ch. 1 §4 (lightness of diet). [Passage](/works/unknown-vratisasana-d0fe75/01/translation/#p-fc4101).
[^4]: Yama Smṛti, Ch. 1 (gruel penance). [Passage](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-00038f).
[^5]: Andreas, Ch. 1 (narrative cannibal law). [Passage](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-a8aa9a).
[^6]: Nouveau traité d'économie I, Ch. 2 (incidental peasant diet). [Passage](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-d7cc02).
[^7]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-6702a2](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-6702a2), [p-86bddc](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-86bddc).
[^8]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 5, [p-9ac8b8](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-9ac8b8) ("eater of fish"); Ch. 4 [p-8b8ea4](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-8b8ea4) (ancestor satisfaction).
[^9]: Vratisaṃśana, Ch. 1, [p-fc4101](/works/unknown-vratisasana-d0fe75/01/translation/#p-fc4101).
[^10]: Yama Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-00038f](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-00038f).
[^11]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-1db5fd](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-1db5fd).
[^12]: Kātyāyana Smṛti, Ch. 11 [p-4fc7cc](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/11/translation/#p-4fc7cc); Ch. 68 [p-ecceef](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/68/translation/#p-ecceef).
[^13]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, Ch. 22 [p-a53817](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/22/translation/#p-a53817).
[^14]: Parāśara Smṛti, Ch. 6 [p-aa2384](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/06/translation/#p-aa2384).
[^15]: Vīramitrodaya, Ch. 1 [p-fc3a75](/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-fc3a75).
[^16]: Andreas, Ch. 1 [p-a8aa9a](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-a8aa9a).
